topic: I commute on the interstate every morning and a grey sedan has been two cars ahead of me for eight months with my dead grandfather's scrapped license plate
title: A Grey Sedan Has Been Ahead of Me Every Morning for 8 Months. The Plate Belonged to My Dead Grandfather. | True Scary Story
description_intro: I commute an hour and ten minutes each way on Interstate seventy six. A grey sedan has been two cars ahead of me every morning for eight months. The license plate matches a nineteen eighty seven Buick LeSabre my grandfather sold for scrap in nineteen ninety four. I watched the crusher fold it flat. I was nine years old.
description:
I commute an hour and ten minutes each way on Interstate seventy six. A grey sedan has been two cars ahead of me every morning for eight months. The license plate matches a nineteen eighty seven Buick LeSabre my grandfather sold for scrap in nineteen ninety four. I watched the crusher fold it flat. I was nine years old.

I noticed the plate in my dashcam footage last September. I bought a second higher resolution camera. The plate captured cleanly across eight frames. Ohio Charlie Mike Whiskey four seven two. White lettering on a sunset background. The exact format Ohio used from nineteen eighty four to nineteen ninety eight. The format itself is retired. The Ohio BMV confirmed the plate was retired in nineteen ninety four and that no vehicle should be carrying it.

There is a detail about what was on the bench seat of that sedan that I have only told one person about. I now drive an hour and ten minutes out of my way to avoid the highway. Watch to the end. My mother showed me a photograph last Sunday I had never seen before.

#scarystories #truescarystories #horrornarration #analoghorror #scarystoriestotellinthedark #truehorrorstories #creepypasta #dashcamhorror
tags: scary stories to tell in the dark

sections.narration: I commute an hour and ten minutes each way on Interstate seventy six. A grey sedan has been two cars ahead of me every morning for eight months. Late nineteen eighties model. Rust at the rear quarter panel. License plate Ohio Charlie Mike Whiskey four seven two. I noticed it for the first time in late September. I have run the plate through every public database I can reach. It is retired. The car attached to the plate was a nineteen eighty seven Buick LeSabre that my grandfather sold for scrap in nineteen ninety four. I watched the crusher fold it flat. I was nine years old.

sections.narration: Interstate seventy six runs from Akron east toward Pennsylvania. The stretch I drive is two lanes in each direction with one rest stop and three exits over forty seven miles. The traffic is heavy at six AM. I take the same on ramp every morning at five fifty two from a town called Marsh Lake. I take the same off ramp at seven oh four near the office in Youngstown. The car in question is always two cars ahead of me by the time I clear the on ramp. It is always two cars ahead when I take the off ramp. It does not seem to enter or exit. It is simply already there.

sections.narration: I installed a dashcam in twenty twenty three after a deer hit. The footage goes to a small SD card that overwrites every four days. My insurance carrier requires me to keep the camera running for any claim. I do not watch the footage. I am supposed to. I never do. Last September the camera flagged an unusual frame because of a brake light pattern ahead. The system pushed a notification to my phone. I opened the clip out of curiosity. The grey sedan was in it. The plate was clearly visible. I recognized the plate the same way I would recognize a phone number I dialed every day as a child.

sections.narration: My grandfather drove a nineteen eighty seven Buick LeSabre from the year he bought it new until the year he was no longer permitted to drive. The car had a salvage history after a snow ditch incident in nineteen ninety three. The plate was the original plate from nineteen eighty seven. He kept the plate when the car went to the scrapyard. He kept it in a kitchen drawer in his apartment until he passed in two thousand and one. The drawer is in my mother's basement now. The plate is still in it. I have walked downstairs and checked. I have held it in my hands. I have read the digits aloud while standing on the dashcam footage at the same time.

sections.narration: I assumed the dashcam had misread the plate. License plate recognition is unreliable. The sedan ahead of me could have a similar plate from a different state. I drove to a service station three exits down the highway and bought a second dashcam with higher resolution. I mounted it the next morning. The grey sedan was ahead of me. The new camera captured the plate cleanly in eight separate frames. Ohio. Charlie Mike Whiskey four seven two. White lettering on a sunset background. The exact format of the plate Ohio used from nineteen eighty four to nineteen ninety eight. The format itself is retired. There is no living plate that uses it. I drove to the office shaking.

sections.narration: I called the Ohio Bureau of Motor Vehicles that afternoon. The clerk pulled the plate from her system. She told me it was a retired plate. She told me it had been retired in nineteen ninety four. She told me the last registered owner had been a man named Earl Pickett. That was my grandfather. I asked her if the plate could have been reissued. She told me Ohio does not reissue retired sunset plates. I asked her if any vehicle was currently authorized to carry it. She paused for a long time and then said no vehicle should be carrying it. She asked me if I had seen one. I told her I had been seeing one every morning for eight months.

sections.narration: I left the office early the next Tuesday. I rejoined the highway at the on ramp where the sedan first appeared in my morning footage. The grey sedan was already two cars ahead. I let the cars between us peel off at the rest stop. I closed the distance. I followed it for nine miles. It took an exit I had passed every day for twelve years and never used. The exit dropped me on a state route through farmland toward a town I had never heard of. The sedan turned into the parking lot of an auto body shop with the sign torn off the front. There were no other cars in the lot. The shop was clearly closed and had been for years.

sections.narration: I parked across the street. The sedan was still running. I could hear the idle from where I sat. I walked across the road. The driver door opened from the inside. There was nobody behind it. I looked into the cabin. The bench seat was the original red cloth my grandfather had it in. There was a roadmap folded on the passenger seat. The map was open to a page showing Marsh Lake. There was a coffee mug from my grandfather's kitchen in the cup holder. The mug had a small chip on the rim shaped like a thumb print. I have known that chip my whole life. I closed the door. The engine kept running. I walked back to my car.

sections.narration: On my way home I stopped at a diner on the same county road. I sat at the counter and waited for my heart to slow. The man at the booth behind me wore a brown wool coat my grandfather had worn the entire winter of nineteen ninety. The coat had a small cigarette burn on the left cuff. I did not turn around for ten minutes. When I finally did, the booth was empty. The plate on the table was eaten clean. There was no waitress on shift who remembered serving him. The bill had not been paid. The check had a tip in cash. The handwriting on the tip line was the handwriting from my grandfather's birthday cards.
image_prompts: wide interior of a small rural diner at late afternoon, a single man seated at the counter from behind, several empty booths along the window, horizontal 16:9 framing

close shot of an old birthday card with the same handwriting visible inside, the card slightly yellowed, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: I now drive the long way to work. The route adds an hour and ten minutes each direction. I have asked my supervisor to move me onto the late shift so the new commute starts later. He agreed. The dashcam footage from the new commute is clean. No grey sedan. No plate. My mother asked me last Sunday why I no longer take the highway. I did not tell her the truth. She showed me a photograph in her album the same evening. It was my grandfather at the wheel of the LeSabre in nineteen ninety. Behind him, in the rear view mirror, you can see another car following him. A small grey sedan. The plate is mine.
